title: ReLU: The Modern Default
concept: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) is a strikingly simple activation function: it outputs 0 for negative inputs, and the input itself unchanged for positive inputs — and it's the most common activation function in modern networks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what range does Sigmoid squash any input into?
exercises.explain: Right — Sigmoid always lands between 0 and 1. Now meet an activation function that barely does any math at all.

cards.title: The activation function that does almost nothing
cards.body: Sigmoid and Tanh both involve smooth, curvy math to squash numbers down. ReLU throws that out. For half of all possible inputs, it does literally nothing at all — and it's still the most popular activation function in modern deep learning.

cards.title: ReLU: chop off the negatives
cards.body: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) follows one simple rule: if the input is negative, output 0. If the input is positive (or zero), output that exact same number, completely unchanged. No smooth curve — just a sharp bend at zero.

cards.title: A one-way valve
cards.body: Think of ReLU like a one-way valve on a pipe: it blocks anything trying to flow backward (negative numbers become 0), but lets forward flow (positive numbers) through at full, unchanged pressure.

cards.title: Running numbers through ReLU
cards.body: Input -5 → output 0. Input 0 → output 0. Input 3 → output 3. Input 42 → output 42. Negative numbers get chopped to zero; positive numbers pass through untouched.

cards.title: Simple doesn't mean weak
cards.body: You might assume something this simple — just chop off the negatives — must be a weak, toy activation function. In reality, ReLU is the default choice in most modern large networks, because it's fast to compute and trains deep networks more reliably than Sigmoid or Tanh.

cards.title: Smooth curve vs. sharp bend
cards.body: Two very different shapes, two different trade-offs:

right.text:
Sharp bend at zero
Fast, simple, most common today

exercises.prompt: What does ReLU output when given a negative input, like -7?
exercises.explain: Correct — ReLU chops any negative input down to exactly 0.

exercises.prompt: True or false: ReLU outputs the exact same number it receives, whenever that number is positive.
exercises.explain: True! For positive inputs, ReLU passes the number through completely unchanged.

exercises.prompt: A neuron computes a weighted sum of 12. What does that neuron output after applying ReLU?
exercises.explain: Since 12 is positive, ReLU passes it through completely unchanged.

exercises.prompt: You're training an enormous network with millions of neurons and need every activation calculation to be as fast as possible. Which activation function is the modern industry default for exactly this reason, and why?

options.text: ReLU, because its rule (chop negatives, pass positives through) is far simpler and faster to compute than Sigmoid or Tanh's smooth curves
Sigmoid, because probabilities are always the fastest thing to compute
Tanh, because negative outputs make training faster
None of them — speed doesn't depend on the activation function

exercises.explain: Exactly! ReLU's simplicity makes it the go-to choice for speed at scale. There's one more activation function worth knowing — one that doesn't work neuron-by-neuron, but across a whole layer at once.
